one_line_takeaway: A frontier LLM acting as an autonomous ML engineer -- generating synthetic training/validation data, LoRA-fine-tuning, and iteratively hard-negative-mining a small on-device student model with no human labels -- beats a matched-compute DPO baseline by a wide margin (up to 94.31% vs 50-80% accuracy) on SMS smishing detection, but which teacher LLM does the teaching swings final accuracy by more than 25 percentage points.
key_finding: Agentic knowledge distillation substantially and consistently outperforms both zero-shot and DPO baselines, but its effectiveness depends heavily on which teacher LLM is used. Zero-shot (Table I): Qwen2.5-0.5B is near-useless (49.80% accuracy, 0.27% recall — correctly identifies only 2/747 spam messages); SmolLM2-135M is near-random (46.85% accuracy, 45.11% recall). DPO baseline (Table II): modest, highly variable gains — Qwen2.5-0.5B stays near-chance across all four teachers (51.3-52.7% accuracy); SmolLM2-135M benefits more, topping out at 80.25% accuracy / 80.01% F1 with Claude-generated preference data; DPO required ~1.7-2.1M tokens per teacher. Agentic knowledge distillation (Table III): best configuration is Claude Opus 4.5 teaching Qwen2.5-0.5B — 94.31% accuracy, 92.65% precision, 96.25% recall, 94.42% F1, using only 27.91K tokens in ~7 minutes. Across teachers, final accuracy ranged from 59.71% (GPT 5.2 Codex -> SmolLM2-135M) up to 94.31%, a >25 percentage-point spread attributed to teacher LLM quality as autonomous ML engineer. Per Table IV, accuracy improvement over the zero-shot baseline ranged from +12.86pp (GPT 5.2 Codex, SmolLM2) to +44.51pp (Claude Opus 4.5, Qwen2.5-0.5B). Claude Opus 4.5 and DeepSeek V3.2 produced the most balanced classifiers (Claude: 86-94% accuracy, 80-93% precision, ~96% recall; DeepSeek: 86-92% accuracy with precision and recall both in the 83-93% range, e.g. Qwen2.5-0.5B at 92.10% accuracy/92.13% F1 and SmolLM2-135M at 86.21% accuracy/85.75% F1). Gemini 3 Pro was intermediate (80-85% accuracy, 73-79% precision, 95-97% recall) and was the most token-efficient teacher (14-17K tokens vs. 24-33K for others), yet was still outperformed by Claude and DeepSeek, indicating token count alone does not predict success. GPT 5.2 Codex showed the sharpest precision-recall imbalance: for Qwen2.5-0.5B it produced 98.76% precision but only 42.70% recall (missing over half of spam); for SmolLM2-135M it flipped to 99.87% recall but only 55.38% precision (flagging nearly half of legitimate messages as spam). The paper also reports that for Claude+Qwen2.5-0.5B, the targeted hard-negative refinement phase reduced false positives from 207 to 57. A notable secondary finding: the full agentic loop used far fewer tokens (15-33K total) than the single-stage DPO baseline (1.7-2.1M tokens), i.e., closed-loop targeted refinement was both more accurate and far cheaper in token budget than static large-scale preference data generation.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (stated explicitly as two numbered contributions) is: (1) 'Agentic Knowledge Distillation' (AKD), a closed-loop framework in which a frontier teacher LLM acts as an autonomous ML engineer to fine-tune a small on-device student model for SMS spam/smishing detection — the teacher generates a synthetic training set and a separate held-out synthetic validation set from its own parametric knowledge, LoRA-fine-tunes the student, evaluates on the synthetic validation set only, hypothesizes failure patterns (e.g. high FP rate implying legitimate messages misclassified as spam), generates targeted 'hard negative'-style synthetic examples to address those hypothesized weaknesses, and repeats until validation metrics plateau, with the real human-labelled test set strictly air-gapped from the loop and used only for a final post-hoc check; and (2) an empirical study across 4 teacher LLMs x 2 student SLMs showing that teacher LLM choice is a critical determinant of final student performance (a >25 percentage-point accuracy spread between best and worst teacher). Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) is explicitly NOT part of the novel contribution: the paper uses DPO (citing Rafailov et al. [21], NeurIPS 2023) only as a controlled non-agentic baseline — same teacher LLMs, same synthetic domain knowledge, same LoRA configuration — to isolate what the closed-loop agentic refinement adds over a single-stage static preference-tuning pipeline.
threat_model: Defensive/detection framing, not an attack paper. Threat: SMS-based phishing ('smishing') — phishing links, fake delivery/banking alerts, cryptocurrency scams, lottery fraud, premium-rate abuse, social engineering — delivered via SMS, which the authors state has 'increased by over 300% in recent years' (citing a Proofpoint 2024 report) and which evades many traditional filters while benefiting from higher user trust than email. The deployment constraint driving the design is on-device/edge inference: cloud-based LLM filtering of personal SMS content raises privacy, latency, and cost concerns, motivating distillation into small (100M-500M parameter) student models runnable on consumer hardware (their experiments use a Mac Mini M4, 16GB unified memory) without sending message content to external APIs. The paper explicitly flags a dual-use/misuse risk as its own caveat (not researcher-added embellishment): the same technique could in principle be used to train models that generate convincing phishing messages or that evade detection, though the authors note (as their own hedge, not a demonstrated result) that generating effective attacks is likely harder than detecting them. No adversarial robustness, evasion, or red-teaming experiments are conducted against the resulting classifiers — the threat model addressed is purely 'can we build an accurate on-device smishing classifier without human-labelled data,' not 'is this classifier robust to adaptive adversaries.'
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors: (1) performance is bounded by the teacher LLM's parametric knowledge of the task domain — emerging attack patterns absent from the teacher's training data may be missed entirely, since no real threat data is ever used; (2) although the agent never sees raw test examples, it does require aggregate evaluation metrics on the synthetic validation set to guide iteration, so the loop is not fully 'blind'; (3) synthetic validation 'ground truth' is generated by the same teacher LLM being evaluated, so validation quality inherits any blind spots or biases in that teacher's domain knowledge (self-graded evaluation, not independently verified); (4) no adversarial-robustness or evasion testing is performed on the resulting classifiers, and the dual-use risk (misuse for generating evasive/convincing phishing) is raised only as a hedge, not investigated empirically; (5) evaluation is on a single public, fairly old (2011) benchmark (SMS Spam Collection) rebalanced to 1,494 messages, so generalization to more recent/adversarial smishing campaigns is untested; (6) only two small student architectures and four teacher LLMs are tested, and the GPT 5.2 Codex results show the approach can fail dramatically (severe precision/recall imbalance) when the teacher generates imbalanced or insufficiently diverse synthetic data, meaning the framework's reliability is not guaranteed across arbitrary teacher choices.

datasets_used: SMS Spam Collection (Almeida, Hidalgo & Yamakami, DocEng 2011) — public 5,574-message corpus aggregating Grumbletext UK forum (425 spam), NUS SMS Corpus (3,375 ham), Caroline Tag's PhD thesis (450 ham), and SMS Spam Corpus v0.1; authors construct a balanced 1,494-message subset (747 spam / 747 ham) used strictly as a held-out final test set, never seen during training/iteration

Teacher-LLM-generated synthetic training set: 2,000+ examples per run (plus targeted refinement additions across iterations), 50/50 spam/ham, covering phishing, smishing, fake delivery alerts, crypto scams, lottery fraud, premium-rate abuse, and benign casual/workplace/service messages
Teacher-LLM-generated synthetic validation set: 500 examples, fixed at the start of each run, used only for internal loop feedback (never fed real labels)
Teacher-LLM-generated DPO preference dataset: 10,000 (prompt, preferred label, dispreferred label) samples per teacher LLM
quantitative_results: Zero-shot baseline (Table I): Qwen2.5-0.5B 49.80% accuracy / 28.57% precision / 0.27% recall / 0.53% F1; SmolLM2-135M 46.85% accuracy / 46.74% precision / 45.11% recall / 46.00% F1

DPO baseline best case (Table II): Claude Opus 4.5 -> SmolLM2-135M, 80.25% accuracy / 81.81% precision / 80.25% recall / 80.01% F1; Qwen2.5-0.5B stays 51-53% accuracy across all four teachers under DPO
Agentic KD best configuration (Table III): Claude Opus 4.5 -> Qwen2.5-0.5B, 94.31% accuracy / 92.65% precision / 96.25% recall / 94.42% F1, 27.91K tokens, ~7 minutes
Agentic KD, GPT 5.2 Codex -> Qwen2.5-0.5B: 71.08% accuracy / 98.76% precision / 42.70% recall / 59.64% F1
Agentic KD, GPT 5.2 Codex -> SmolLM2-135M: 59.71% accuracy / 55.38% precision / 99.87% recall / 71.26% F1
Agentic KD, DeepSeek V3.2 -> Qwen2.5-0.5B: 92.10% accuracy / 91.77% precision / 92.50% recall / 92.13% F1 (31.2K tokens, ~8 min)
Agentic KD, DeepSeek V3.2 -> SmolLM2-135M: 86.21% accuracy / 88.70% precision / 83.00% recall / 85.75% F1 (32.1K tokens, ~7 min)
Agentic KD, Gemini 3 Pro -> Qwen2.5-0.5B: 85.21% accuracy / 79.29% precision / 95.31% recall / 86.58% F1 (14.83K tokens, ~9 min)
Agentic KD, Gemini 3 Pro -> SmolLM2-135M: 80.46% accuracy / 72.73% precision / 97.46% recall / 83.31% F1 (16.78K tokens, ~8 min)

Accuracy improvement over zero-shot baseline (Table IV): Claude Opus 4.5 +44.51pp (Qwen2.5-0.5B) / +39.43pp (SmolLM2-135M); DeepSeek V3.2 +42.30pp / +39.36pp; Gemini 3 Pro +35.41pp / +33.61pp; GPT 5.2 Codex +21.28pp / +12.86pp
False positives for Claude Opus 4.5 -> Qwen2.5-0.5B reduced from 207 to 57 after hard-negative refinement
Token cost asymmetry: agentic KD used 15-33K tokens total vs. DPO baseline's 1.7-2.1M tokens per teacher
baselines_compared: Zero-shot prompting of both untuned student SLMs (Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct, SmolLM2-135M-Instruct) — lower-bound baseline

Teacher-LLM-generated Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO, Rafailov et al. 2023) with identical LoRA configuration and same synthetic domain knowledge as the agentic method, but single offline stage with no iterative feedback or targeted refinement — the paper's controlled ablation baseline for isolating the value of closed-loop agentic refinement
